aliases: Egg fried rice omelette with meat

english: Egg fried rice omelette with meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for omurice exists. FOODON_00002572 'fried rice food product' is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Omurice is a Japanese dish of ketchup-seasoned fried rice with meat wrapped in a thin egg omelette; the fried rice component is the dominant carbohydrate mass.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Omelette) is used as the closest available anchor; no dedicated omurice code exists in FoodEx2. The dish combines an omelette wrapper with a fried rice and meat filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for omurice or Japanese omelette rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of omurice with meat is approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of ketchup-seasoned fried rice with diced meat wrapped in or topped with a thin egg omelette.

nutrient_source.title: Omurice (Egg Rice) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo entry for Omurice (with meat addition) used as primary source. The input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. The food_group and food_subgroup in the input ('Fish and seafood / Fish') appear to be a data-entry error; corrected to 'Mixed / Rice-based mixed dish' based on the dish's actual composition. All nutrient values are preserved from the HPB-sourced input.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for omurice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food 'white rice, cooked' (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate component. The egg omelette wrapper and added meat contribute negligible carbohydrate and modestly lower the effective GI through protein-fat co-ingestion. GL per 350 g serving calculated as: (65 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 = 57.3; GL of 37.2 uses a conservative 200 g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion to account for egg and meat dilution effect, documented as an estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; potassium 327 mg/100 g partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium (~35% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so patients should request reduced ketchup sauce, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and limit portion size to one serving per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (25.19 g/100 g) from white rice base with medium GI (~65); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g partially moderates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 88 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~37, which is high; patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request brown rice substitution where available, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100 g from egg and meat; sodium 231 mg/100 g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB source (likely a rounding artefact given egg content)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Omurice with meat contains moderate saturated fat from egg and meat components and borderline sodium; cardiovascular patients should choose lean chicken over red or processed meat, request reduced ketchup to lower sodium, and limit consumption to once or twice per week as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato ketchup (nightshade); meat type influences arachidonic acid load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade alkaloids should request reduced or no ketchup; if the meat component is red meat or pork, the arachidonic acid (omega-6) load increases pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production, so chicken is the preferred meat choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Omurice with Meat (オムライス 肉入り; 蛋包饭加肉) is a Japanese-origin comfort dish widely popular across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. It consists of ketchup-seasoned fried rice mixed with diced meat — typically chicken, pork, or beef — and mixed vegetables such as onion, carrot, and peas, all wrapped in or topped with a thin, lightly cooked egg omelette. The dish is finished with a drizzle of tomato ketchup or demi-glace sauce. The texture contrasts a soft, yielding egg exterior with the slightly chewy, savory rice interior. The flavor profile is mildly sweet-savory from the ketchup, with umami depth from the meat and egg. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 3.7 g dietary fibre and 231 mg sodium. The white rice base gives a medium glycemic index (~65), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The potassium content (327 mg/100 g) partially offsets the sodium load under DASH dietary principles. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from ketchup and seasoning, moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to the high carbohydrate load from white rice, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from egg and meat. It is suitable for general healthy adults in moderate portions and is a common lunch or dinner item in Japanese restaurants, school canteens, and family restaurants across Singapore and Japan.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. The food identity is well-established. The food_group and food_subgroup have been corrected from the erroneous input values ('Fish and seafood / Fish') to the accurate 'Mixed / Rice-based mixed dish'. GI is estimated from the analogous white rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest approved source.
notes: 1. Input food_group was 'Fish and seafood' and food_subgroup was 'Fish' — these are clearly erroneous for an omurice dish and have been corrected to 'Mixed' and 'Rice-based mixed dish' respectively. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100 g based on typical omurice composition (egg yolk and meat fat contribution); this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and is flagged as an estimate. 3. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific omurice preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026, and no supplementary approved source provides these values for this dish: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — vitamin profile not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — mineral fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. Source consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), accessed 2026-04-16. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is likely a rounding artefact given the egg and meat content of the dish, but the HPB-reported value is preserved as-is per source fidelity rules. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct omurice GI measurement was found in approved sources. GL of 37.2 uses a conservative 200 g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion to account for the egg and meat dilution effect; this is documented as an estimate. 6. The dish is not inherently halal-certified as meat type is unspecified; halal status depends on the specific restaurant or preparation.
